questions.q: Reverse a string WITHOUT using a built-in Reverse(). Implement it yourself.

answer.oneLiner: Convert to a char array and swap from both ends toward the middle with two pointers. O(n) time, O(n) space (strings are immutable in C#).
keyPoints: Two pointers: `left` at start, `right` at end.
Swap, then move both inward until they cross.
Strings are immutable, so work on a `char[]`.

code.note: O(n) time, O(n) space for the char array

followUps: Reverse in place over a char[] (then it's O(1) extra space).
How would you reverse only the words, not the characters?
Handle Unicode surrogate pairs / combining characters?

questions.q: Given an integer array of size n, find all elements that appear more than ⌊n/3⌋ times. Constraints: 1 <= n <= 5*10^4, -10^9 <= nums[i] <= 10^9. Try O(n) time and O(1) space.

answer.oneLiner: Boyer-Moore voting extended to n/3: there can be at most two elements above ⌊n/3⌋, so track two candidates with two counters, then a second pass verifies their real counts. O(n) time, O(1) space.
keyPoints: Above n/3 means at most 2 winners (3 would need >n votes).
Pass 1: maintain (cand1,count1) and (cand2,count2) via voting.
Pass 2: re-count both candidates and keep only those > n/3.
Match an existing candidate first, then fill an empty one, else decrement both.

code.note: O(n) time, O(1) space

followUps: Why are there at most 2 answers for the n/3 case?
Generalize to elements appearing more than n/k times (k candidates).
What if you used a Dictionary instead -> O(n) space but simpler.

questions.q: Count how many times each element occurs in an array using a Dictionary.

answer.oneLiner: Iterate once and accumulate counts in a Dictionary<T,int>. O(n) time, O(k) space where k is the number of distinct values.
keyPoints: Dictionary key = element, value = running count.
`TryGetValue` or the `dict[x] = dict.GetValueOrDefault(x) + 1` idiom.
Average O(1) per lookup/insert.

code.note: O(n) time, O(k) space

followUps: LINQ one-liner: nums.GroupBy(x => x).ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => g.Count()).
How to get the most frequent element from this?

questions.q: Find all duplicate elements in an array.

answer.oneLiner: Walk the array using a HashSet of seen values; if Add returns false, it's a duplicate. O(n) time, O(n) space.
keyPoints: `HashSet.Add` returns false when the value already exists.
Use a second set for results to avoid reporting a value twice.
One pass, average O(1) per operation.

code.note: O(n) time, O(n) space

followUps: If values are in range [1..n], can you do it in O(1) extra space by index marking?
Return the count of each duplicate too.

questions.q: Find the second largest element in an array in a single pass.

answer.oneLiner: Track the largest and second-largest as you scan once; update both correctly on each element. O(n) time, O(1) space.
keyPoints: Initialize both to long.MinValue (handles int.MinValue inputs).
If x > first: second = first; first = x.
Else if x > second and x != first: second = x.
Guard against arrays with fewer than 2 distinct values.

code.note: O(n) time, O(1) space

followUps: Should duplicates of the max count as second largest?
Generalize to the k-th largest (heap or QuickSelect).

questions.q: Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array.

answer.oneLiner: Two pointers, one per array; repeatedly take the smaller front element into the result, then drain the remainder. O(m+n) time, O(m+n) space.
keyPoints: This is the merge step of merge sort.
Compare a[i] vs b[j], copy the smaller, advance that pointer.
After the loop, copy whatever is left in either array.

code.note: O(m+n) time, O(m+n) space; stable

followUps: Merge in place when one array has trailing capacity (fill from the back).
How does this relate to the overall O(n log n) of merge sort?

questions.q: Given a sentence, reverse the order of the words and count how many times each word occurs.

answer.oneLiner: Split on whitespace, reverse the word array (or iterate back to front) to rebuild the sentence, and tally counts in a Dictionary. O(n) time, O(w) space.
keyPoints: `Split` with RemoveEmptyEntries to ignore extra spaces.
Reverse the array, then String.Join to rebuild.
Count case-insensitively if required (use a case-insensitive comparer).

code.note: O(n) time over characters, O(w) space for word map

followUps: Reverse the words without Array.Reverse (two-pointer swap).
Reverse characters within each word as well.
Return the most frequent word.

questions.q: Generate the first n numbers of the Fibonacci series iteratively.

answer.oneLiner: Keep the last two values and roll forward in a loop, no recursion. O(n) time, O(1) space (O(n) if you store the whole series).
keyPoints: Iterative avoids the exponential blowup of naive recursion.
Track `a` and `b`, update `(a, b) = (b, a + b)`.
Use `long` to delay overflow.

code.note: O(n) time, O(1) extra space

followUps: Why is naive recursion O(2^n)? How does memoization fix it?
Compute the n-th Fibonacci in O(log n) (matrix exponentiation).

questions.q: Print all prime numbers up to n efficiently.

answer.oneLiner: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes: mark multiples of each prime as composite starting from p*p. O(n log log n) time, O(n) space.
keyPoints: Boolean array; index = number, false = still prime.
Start crossing out at p*p (smaller multiples already crossed).
Only loop p up to sqrt(n).

code.note: O(n log log n) time, O(n) space

followUps: Trial division check for a single number (O(sqrt n)).
Segmented sieve for very large ranges to bound memory.

questions.q: Count the number of trailing zeros in n! (factorial of n).

answer.oneLiner: Trailing zeros come from factors of 10 = 2*5, and 5s are scarcer than 2s, so just count factors of 5: n/5 + n/25 + n/125 + ... O(log n) time, O(1) space.
keyPoints: Never compute n! itself (overflows fast).
Each multiple of 5 contributes one 5; multiples of 25 contribute an extra, etc.
Sum floor(n / 5^k) until the term is 0.

code.note: O(log_5 n) time, O(1) space

followUps: Why count 5s and not 2s?
Count trailing zeros in base b (factor b into primes).

questions.q: What sorting algorithm is preferred for linked lists, and why?

answer.oneLiner: Merge sort: it's O(n log n), doesn't need random access, and merges by relinking nodes, so it works on lists with O(1) extra space (bottom-up) unlike quicksort which relies on indexing.
keyPoints: Quicksort/heapsort need O(1) random access -> bad for lists.
Merge sort only walks nodes sequentially and splices pointers.
Stable and a guaranteed O(n log n) (no worst-case degradation like quicksort).
Bottom-up merge sort avoids recursion stack -> O(1) auxiliary space.

code.note: O(n log n) time; relinks nodes, no random access

followUps: Why is quicksort poor for linked lists?
How does bottom-up merge sort reach O(1) extra space?

questions.q: Compare singly, doubly, and circular linked lists. Which would you use to implement browser tab/page switching, and why?

answer.oneLiner: Singly = one forward pointer; doubly = prev+next (O(1) bidirectional move/delete); circular = tail links back to head. For browser back/forward navigation use a doubly linked list (move both directions in O(1)); for round-robin tab cycling a circular list fits.
keyPoints: Singly: less memory, forward traversal only, O(n) to find prev.
Doubly: prev+next pointers, O(1) insert/delete given a node, both directions.
Circular: last node points to first -> natural cycling / round-robin.
Back/forward history = doubly linked (or two stacks); endless tab cycling = circular.

code.note: Doubly linked -> O(1) Back/Forward

followUps: When would two stacks be simpler than a doubly linked list here?
How does an LRU cache combine a doubly linked list with a hash map?
Trade-off: extra prev pointer memory vs O(1) reverse traversal.

questions.q: What is the difference between a Tuple and a List in C#? When would you use each?

answer.oneLiner: A Tuple is a fixed-size, heterogeneous group of values (different types, accessed by position/name); a List<T> is a resizable, homogeneous sequence (one type, indexable, growable). Use a tuple to return multiple values; use a list for collections you iterate or grow.
keyPoints: Tuple: fixed length, mixed types, great for returning multiple values.
List<T>: dynamic length, single element type, index + iterate.
ValueTuple `(int, string)` is a value type with named members; List<T> is a reference type.
Tuples are not meant to be iterated; lists are.

code.note: Tuple = fixed heterogeneous; List = dynamic homogeneous

followUps: ValueTuple vs the older System.Tuple reference type?
When prefer a small record/class over a tuple for readability?

questions.q: Explain the difference between the stack and the heap in .NET memory management.

answer.oneLiner: The stack is a fast, LIFO region for method call frames, local value types, and references, automatically reclaimed when a method returns; the heap is a larger region for reference-type objects, managed by the garbage collector. Value types live on the stack (or inline), reference types live on the heap.
keyPoints: Stack: per-thread, LIFO, very fast, holds locals/parameters/return addresses; freed on return.
Heap: shared, holds objects (class instances, arrays); cleaned by the GC.
A reference-type variable sits on the stack but points to heap data.
Boxing moves a value type onto the heap (allocation cost).

code.note: Stack: auto-freed locals; Heap: GC-managed objects

followUps: What causes a StackOverflowException vs an OutOfMemoryException?
How do generational GC (gen 0/1/2) and the LOH work?
Why can stackalloc / Span<T> reduce heap pressure?

questions.q: Write a query to find the second highest salary from an Employee(Id, Name, Salary) table.

answer.oneLiner: Rank distinct salaries with DENSE_RANK() descending and pick rank 2; this handles ties cleanly and returns NULL when there's no second salary.
keyPoints: DENSE_RANK gives equal salaries the same rank (no gaps).
Filter the ranked result to rank = 2.
Subquery approach (MAX where salary < overall MAX) also works.

code.snippet:
-- Using DENSE_RANK
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighestSalary
FROM (
    SELECT Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS rnk
    FROM Employee
) t
WHERE rnk = 2;

-- Equivalent subquery form (returns NULL if none)
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighestSalary
FROM Employee
WHERE Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee);
code.note: DENSE_RANK handles duplicate salaries correctly

followUps: Why DENSE_RANK over ROW_NUMBER here (ties)?
What does each return when only one salary exists?

questions.q: Generalize: write a query (or function) to find the N-th highest salary.

answer.oneLiner: Use DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) and filter rnk = N; wrap in a function so N is a parameter. O(n log n) due to the sort.
keyPoints: Same DENSE_RANK pattern, parameterized by N.
OFFSET/FETCH also works but doesn't dedupe ties.
Return NULL gracefully when fewer than N distinct salaries exist.

code.note: Sort-bound: O(n log n)

followUps: How would OFFSET @N-1 ROWS FETCH NEXT 1 differ with duplicate salaries?
Per-department N-th highest (PARTITION BY DepartmentId)?

questions.q: Give an example of a correlated subquery and explain how it differs from a plain subquery.

answer.oneLiner: A correlated subquery references a column from the outer query, so it is logically re-evaluated per outer row; here we find employees earning more than their own department's average.
keyPoints: Inner query depends on the outer row (the correlation, e.g. e.DeptId).
Plain (non-correlated) subqueries run once, independently.
Often rewritable as a JOIN / window function for performance.

code.snippet:
-- Correlated: inner query uses outer alias e
SELECT e.Name, e.Salary, e.DepartmentId
FROM Employee e
WHERE e.Salary > (
    SELECT AVG(e2.Salary)
    FROM Employee e2
    WHERE e2.DepartmentId = e.DepartmentId   -- correlation
);

-- Window-function rewrite (usually faster)
SELECT Name, Salary, DepartmentId
FROM (
    SELECT Name, Salary, DepartmentId,
           AVG(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DepartmentId) AS deptAvg
    FROM Employee
) t
WHERE Salary > deptAvg;
code.note: Correlated subquery references the outer row

followUps: Why can correlated subqueries be slow (row-by-row evaluation)?
When does EXISTS read better than IN here?

questions.q: Write a self join: list each employee alongside their manager's name from an Employee(Id, Name, ManagerId) table.

answer.oneLiner: Join the Employee table to itself with two aliases, matching employee.ManagerId to manager.Id; use a LEFT JOIN so the top boss (NULL manager) is still listed.
keyPoints: Two aliases of the same table: e (employee) and m (manager).
Join condition: e.ManagerId = m.Id.
LEFT JOIN keeps employees with no manager (CEO).

code.snippet:
SELECT e.Name AS Employee,
       m.Name AS Manager
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Employee m ON e.ManagerId = m.Id
ORDER BY e.Name;

-- Sample:
-- Alice (ManagerId NULL) -> Manager: NULL (she's the boss)
-- Bob   (ManagerId = Alice.Id) -> Manager: Alice
code.note: LEFT JOIN keeps the manager-less top of the hierarchy

followUps: INNER vs LEFT join: who gets dropped?
Traverse the full hierarchy with a recursive CTE.
Count direct reports per manager (GROUP BY m.Id).

questions.q: Recyclable and Low Fat Products. Products(product_id PK, low_fats ENUM('Y','N'), recyclable ENUM('Y','N')). Find the ids of products that are both low fat and recyclable.

answer.oneLiner: Filter rows where both flag columns equal 'Y'.

code.note: Basic WHERE with AND

keyPoints: Two equality predicates joined by AND.

questions.q: Find Customer Referee. Customer(id PK, name, referee_id). Report the names of customers that are NOT referred by the customer with id = 2 (note: referee_id can be NULL).

answer.oneLiner: Keep rows where referee_id is not 2, explicitly including NULLs since NULL <> 2 is unknown, not true.

code.note: NULL-aware filtering: <> never matches NULL

keyPoints: NULL <> 2 evaluates to UNKNOWN, so add OR referee_id IS NULL.

questions.q: Big Countries. World(name PK, continent, area, population, gdp). A country is big if area >= 3000000 OR population >= 25000000. Return name, population, area.

answer.oneLiner: Select the three columns where either size threshold is met.

code.note: OR condition on two thresholds

keyPoints: Either condition qualifies, so use OR.

questions.q: Article Views I. Views(article_id, author_id, viewer_id, view_date). Find all authors who viewed at least one of their own articles. Return the distinct ids sorted ascending.

answer.oneLiner: Keep rows where author_id = viewer_id, take DISTINCT ids, order ascending.

code.snippet:
SELECT DISTINCT author_id AS id
FROM Views
WHERE author_id = viewer_id
ORDER BY id;
code.note: DISTINCT + equality predicate

keyPoints: author_id = viewer_id means self-view.
DISTINCT removes duplicate authors.

questions.q: Invalid Tweets. Tweets(tweet_id PK, content). Find the ids of invalid tweets, where a tweet is invalid if the number of characters in content is strictly greater than 15.

answer.oneLiner: Filter by CHAR_LENGTH(content) > 15.

code.note: CHAR_LENGTH counts characters (not bytes like LENGTH)

keyPoints: Prefer CHAR_LENGTH over LENGTH for multibyte safety.

questions.q: Replace Employee ID With The Unique Identifier. Employees(id PK, name), EmployeeUNI(id, unique_id). Show the unique_id of each user; if a user has no unique_id, show NULL.

answer.oneLiner: LEFT JOIN from Employees to EmployeeUNI on id so unmatched employees still appear with NULL unique_id.

code.note: LEFT JOIN preserves rows without a match

keyPoints: LEFT JOIN keeps all employees; missing unique_id becomes NULL.

questions.q: Product Sales Analysis I. Sales(sale_id, product_id, year, quantity, price), Product(product_id PK, product_name). Report product_name, year, and price for each Sales row.

answer.oneLiner: Inner join Sales to Product on product_id and project the requested columns.

code.note: Simple INNER JOIN on FK

keyPoints: Join on product_id to enrich sales with the name.

questions.q: Customer Who Visited but Did Not Make Any Transactions. Visits(visit_id, customer_id), Transactions(transaction_id, visit_id, amount). For each customer, find how many times they visited without making a transaction. Return customer_id, count_no_trans.

answer.oneLiner: LEFT JOIN visits to transactions and keep only visits with no matching transaction (transaction_id IS NULL), then count per customer.

code.snippet:
SELECT v.customer_id, COUNT(*) AS count_no_trans
FROM Visits v
LEFT JOIN Transactions t ON v.visit_id = t.visit_id
WHERE t.transaction_id IS NULL
GROUP BY v.customer_id;
code.note: LEFT JOIN + IS NULL = anti-join

keyPoints: transaction_id IS NULL isolates visits with no transaction.
GROUP BY customer to count them.

questions.q: Rising Temperature. Weather(id PK, recordDate, temperature). Find all dates' ids with a higher temperature than the previous (yesterday's) date.

answer.oneLiner: Self-join Weather to itself where the dates are exactly one day apart and today's temperature exceeds yesterday's.

code.snippet:
SELECT w1.id
FROM Weather w1
JOIN Weather w2
  ON DATEDIFF(w1.recordDate, w2.recordDate) = 1
WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature;
code.note: Self-join on consecutive days via DATEDIFF

keyPoints: DATEDIFF(today, yesterday) = 1 ties consecutive days.
Compare temperatures across the two aliases.

questions.q: Average Time of Process per Machine. Activity(machine_id, process_id, activity_type ENUM('start','end'), timestamp). For each machine compute the average time to complete a process (end timestamp minus start timestamp), rounded to 3 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: Join start rows to end rows on machine_id + process_id and average (end - start) per machine.

code.snippet:
SELECT a.machine_id,
       ROUND(AVG(b.timestamp - a.timestamp), 3) AS processing_time
FROM Activity a
JOIN Activity b
  ON a.machine_id = b.machine_id
 AND a.process_id = b.process_id
 AND a.activity_type = 'start'
 AND b.activity_type = 'end'
GROUP BY a.machine_id;
code.note: Self-join pairing start/end, then AVG + ROUND

keyPoints: Pair each start with its matching end per process.
AVG over all processes; ROUND to 3.

questions.q: Employee Bonus. Employee(empId PK, name, supervisor, salary), Bonus(empId, bonus). Report the name and bonus of employees with a bonus less than 1000, including employees who have no bonus record.

answer.oneLiner: LEFT JOIN to keep bonus-less employees, then filter bonus < 1000 OR bonus IS NULL.

code.snippet:
SELECT e.name, b.bonus
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Bonus b ON e.empId = b.empId
WHERE b.bonus < 1000 OR b.bonus IS NULL;
code.note: LEFT JOIN; IS NULL captures employees with no bonus

keyPoints: Employees with no bonus have NULL, which must be included explicitly.

questions.q: Students and Examinations. Students(student_id, student_name), Subjects(subject_name), Examinations(student_id, subject_name). For every student and every subject, count how many exams the student took. Order by student_id, then subject_name.

answer.oneLiner: CROSS JOIN students with subjects to get every pair, LEFT JOIN examinations, then COUNT the exam rows per pair.

code.snippet:
SELECT s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name,
       COUNT(e.subject_name) AS attended_exams
FROM Students s
CROSS JOIN Subjects sub
LEFT JOIN Examinations e
  ON e.student_id = s.student_id
 AND e.subject_name = sub.subject_name
GROUP BY s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name
ORDER BY s.student_id, sub.subject_name;
code.note: CROSS JOIN builds all pairs; COUNT(col) ignores NULLs

keyPoints: CROSS JOIN generates every student-subject combination.
COUNT(e.subject_name) gives 0 for untaken subjects.

questions.q: Managers with at Least 5 Direct Reports. Employee(id PK, name, department, managerId). Find managers who have at least five direct reports. Return their names.

answer.oneLiner: Group employees by managerId, keep groups with 5+ members, then join back to get the manager's name.

code.snippet:
SELECT name
FROM Employee
WHERE id IN (
    SELECT managerId
    FROM Employee
    GROUP BY managerId
    HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
);
code.note: GROUP BY ... HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5, then map id back to name

keyPoints: HAVING filters aggregated counts.
Subquery returns manager ids; outer query resolves names.

questions.q: Confirmation Rate. Signups(user_id PK, time_stamp), Confirmations(user_id, time_stamp, action ENUM('confirmed','timeout')). The confirmation rate of a user is the number of 'confirmed' messages divided by total requests. Report user_id and confirmation_rate rounded to 2 decimals (0 if no requests).

answer.oneLiner: LEFT JOIN signups to confirmations; AVG of (action='confirmed') gives the rate, wrapped in IFNULL/ROUND for users with no requests.

code.snippet:
SELECT s.user_id,
       ROUND(IFNULL(AVG(c.action = 'confirmed'), 0), 2) AS confirmation_rate
FROM Signups s
LEFT JOIN Confirmations c ON s.user_id = c.user_id
GROUP BY s.user_id;
code.note: AVG of a boolean = fraction true; IFNULL handles no-request users

keyPoints: (c.action = 'confirmed') yields 1/0; AVG = confirmation fraction.
IFNULL(..., 0) covers users with no confirmations.

questions.q: Not Boring Movies. Cinema(id PK, movie, description, rating). Report movies with an odd id and a description that is not 'boring', ordered by rating descending.

answer.oneLiner: Filter id % 2 = 1 and description <> 'boring', then ORDER BY rating DESC.

code.snippet:
SELECT *
FROM Cinema
WHERE id % 2 = 1 AND description <> 'boring'
ORDER BY rating DESC;
code.note: Modulo for odd ids; ORDER BY DESC

keyPoints: id % 2 = 1 selects odd ids.

questions.q: Average Selling Price. Prices(product_id, start_date, end_date, price), UnitsSold(product_id, purchase_date, units). For each product compute the average selling price = SUM(price*units)/SUM(units), rounded to 2 decimals; products with no sales get 0.

answer.oneLiner: LEFT JOIN prices to units sold where the purchase date falls inside the price window, then take the weighted average, defaulting to 0.

code.snippet:
SELECT p.product_id,
       IFNULL(ROUND(SUM(p.price * u.units) / SUM(u.units), 2), 0) AS average_price
FROM Prices p
LEFT JOIN UnitsSold u
  ON p.product_id = u.product_id
 AND u.purchase_date BETWEEN p.start_date AND p.end_date
GROUP BY p.product_id;
code.note: Date-range join + weighted average; IFNULL for no-sales (division by NULL)

keyPoints: Join on purchase_date BETWEEN start and end.
Weighted avg = SUM(price*units)/SUM(units); IFNULL gives 0.

questions.q: Project Employees I. Project(project_id, employee_id), Employee(employee_id PK, name, experience_years). For each project report the average experience years of its employees, rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: Join Project to Employee on employee_id and AVG(experience_years) per project_id.

code.snippet:
SELECT p.project_id,
       ROUND(AVG(e.experience_years), 2) AS average_years
FROM Project p
JOIN Employee e ON p.employee_id = e.employee_id
GROUP BY p.project_id;
code.note: JOIN + AVG + ROUND grouped by project

keyPoints: Group by project_id, average the joined experience values.

questions.q: Percentage of Users Attended a Contest. Users(user_id PK, user_name), Register(contest_id, user_id). For each contest, report the percentage of all users who registered, rounded to 2 decimals. Order by percentage descending, then contest_id ascending.

answer.oneLiner: Count distinct registrants per contest, divide by the total user count (a scalar subquery), and order as required.

code.snippet:
SELECT contest_id,
       ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) * 100.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Users), 2) AS percentage
FROM Register
GROUP BY contest_id
ORDER BY percentage DESC, contest_id ASC;
code.note: Scalar subquery for the denominator (total users)

keyPoints: Denominator is the full user count via a scalar subquery.
Multiply before dividing to keep precision.

questions.q: Queries Quality and Percentage. Queries(query_name, result, position, rating). For each query_name compute quality = AVG(rating/position) and poor_query_percentage = percentage of queries with rating < 3, both rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: Group by query_name; quality is AVG(rating/position) and poor percentage is AVG(rating < 3) * 100.

code.snippet:
SELECT query_name,
       ROUND(AVG(rating / position), 2) AS quality,
       ROUND(AVG(rating < 3) * 100, 2) AS poor_query_percentage
FROM Queries
WHERE query_name IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY query_name;
code.note: AVG(rating < 3) averages a boolean to get the fraction

keyPoints: AVG(rating/position) is the average per-row ratio.
AVG(boolean)*100 gives the poor-query percentage.

questions.q: Nth Highest Salary. Employee(id PK, salary). Write a generic solution to return the Nth highest distinct salary (return NULL if it does not exist).

answer.oneLiner: Rank distinct salaries with DENSE_RANK() descending and pick rank N; equivalently use DISTINCT ... LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1.

code.note: DENSE_RANK dedupes ties; LIMIT/OFFSET works over a DISTINCT set

keyPoints: DENSE_RANK = N targets the Nth distinct salary.
Empty result naturally yields NULL.
